ORIGINALITY.justification: This paper provides a useful empirical perspective by extending Goodfellow et al.’s interpolation visualizations (Section 3.3) to 2D barycentric/bilinear projections and by systematically comparing multiple optimizers and their basins (Sections 4.1–4.3, 4.4). It also explores a modest methodological variant (RK2 augmentation, A.3–A.4) and effects of batch normalization (A.5), and explicitly differentiates itself from prior theoretical and empirical work (Related Work, Conclusions). However, the contributions are primarily empirical observations and visualization refinements rather than fundamentally new theory or algorithms, so the novelty is moderate rather than high.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (Introduction → Related Work → Experimental Setup → Results → Conclusion) and the experimental approach is described clearly (Sections 3–4), but the main contributions are only implicit (research questions in the intro) rather than stated as a concise contribution list. Notation and equations are mostly understandable, but there are inconsistencies and small errors (e.g. the barycentric/bilinear interpolation descriptions in A.1, some equation indexing) and some sections contain duplicated/garbled text (Appendix A.6). Figures and tables are frequently referenced (Table 1, Figures 2–7, etc.) yet are not included here, and the manuscript contains numerous typos and awkward phrasings (e.g. “mimimization”, “supplemantary”, “intializations”, “outpts”) which impede smooth reading.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s central empirical claims (different optimizers find different final weight vectors; optimizer-specific basin geometry; batch‑norm affects surfaces) are directly supported by the presented experiments: interpolations between initial and final weights and pairwise final-weight interpolations (Sec. 3.3, 4.1–4.2) and the output‑distance metric (Eq. 4.1) provide clear evidence of separations in loss and functional disagreement. However, the methodology has notable gaps: the number of random restarts and statistical variability are not reported, hyperparameter tuning and computational-budget parity between methods (e.g. RK2 uses doubled learning rate / two gradients per step) are not fully controlled, and conclusions rely heavily on linear/barycentric projections (A.1) which can overstate barriers in truly high‑dimensional landscapes. The mathematical descriptions (e.g. RK2 augmentation, optimizer formulas in A.2–A.3) are reasonable and not evidently incorrect, but some claims are stronger than warranted by the limited experimental controls and absence of significance analysis.
